items.evidence_text:
Advisor: Okay—when you say seventy-five, is that before taxes, like the gross amount?
Client: Yes, gross. Wait—no, sorry, I’m mixing it up. Gross is about seventy-nine thousand a year.
Advisor: Got it, about $79,000 gross annually. And that’s coming from pensions, right?
Client: Yes, two pensions.
Advisor: Walk me through them—one at a time. What’s the first one like, monthly or weekly?

Advisor: Three thousand even, or closer to, say, thirty-one hundred?
Client: Closer to thirty-one fifty a month, if I’m being honest.
Advisor: Okay, $3,150 per month. And when you say expenses, does that include any debt payments—credit cards, car loan, anything like that?
Client: No car loan. Credit cards I pay off, so… I guess no.
Advisor: Sometimes people mean the balance, not the payment—do you have a monthly minimum you’re carrying, or is it paid in full?

Advisor: Okay—when you say seventy-five, is that before taxes, like the gross amount?
Client: Yes, gross. Wait—no, sorry, I’m mixing it up. Gross is about seventy-nine thousand a year.
Advisor: Got it, about $79,000 gross annually. And that’s coming from pensions, right?
Client: Yes, two pensions.
Advisor: Walk me through them—one at a time. What’s the first one like, monthly or weekly?

Advisor: Okay. Let’s shift to what you’ve saved. Roughly, what do you have in investable accounts—so not counting the house—across bank, brokerage, retirement accounts?
Client: Um… maybe like five hundred thousand? A little over?
Advisor: A little over five hundred—okay. If you had to put a number on it, is it closer to $520,000 or closer to $500,000?
Client: Closer to five-seventeen, I think. About five hundred seventeen thousand, five hundred.
Advisor: About $517,500—thank you. Let me note that down. And we’ll break that into buckets—taxable, IRA/401k, bank-type savings, and any private investments.

Advisor: And then your home—just so we have the full picture—what’s the approximate value of your primary residence?
Client: About three twenty-five, maybe three twenty-six. Around three hundred twenty-five thousand.
Advisor: Okay, about $325,850 for the house. And no mortgage on it, correct?
Client: Correct, no mortgage.
Advisor: Great. Quick tangent—are you getting decent weather where you are, or is it still gloomy?

Advisor: Okay—when you say seventy-five, is that before taxes, like the gross amount?
Client: Yes, gross. Wait—no, sorry, I’m mixing it up. Gross is about seventy-nine thousand a year.
Advisor: Got it, about $79,000 gross annually. And that’s coming from pensions, right?
Client: Yes, two pensions.
Advisor: Walk me through them—one at a time. What’s the first one like, monthly or weekly?

Advisor: Okay—when you say seventy-five, is that before taxes, like the gross amount?
Client: Yes, gross. Wait—no, sorry, I’m mixing it up. Gross is about seventy-nine thousand a year.
Advisor: Got it, about $79,000 gross annually. And that’s coming from pensions, right?
Client: Yes, two pensions.
Advisor: Walk me through them—one at a time. What’s the first one like, monthly or weekly?

Advisor: Okay—when you say seventy-five, is that before taxes, like the gross amount?
Client: Yes, gross. Wait—no, sorry, I’m mixing it up. Gross is about seventy-nine thousand a year.
Advisor: Got it, about $79,000 gross annually. And that’s coming from pensions, right?
Client: Yes, two pensions.
Advisor: Walk me through them—one at a time. What’s the first one like, monthly or weekly?

Advisor: Got it—one adult. Sorry, I’m going to pause you there—when I say “adult,” I mean 18-plus living in the home, not dependents you support financially.
Client: Right, right—then yes, one adult.
Advisor: Perfect. Next—risk comfort. Earlier you sounded pretty cautious. Would you describe yourself as conservative, moderate, or aggressive?
Client: Conservative. I don’t like big swings.
Advisor: Yeah, that tracks with what you’ve been saying. When markets drop, you’d rather protect principal than chase returns, right?

Client: Oh, I’m okay. A little tired, honestly, but I’m glad to get this organized.
Advisor: I hear you. We’ll keep it simple and step-by-step. Tell me a bit about you and what you want help with.
Client: I’m 73, widowed, retired. I just want to make sure I don’t mess anything up and that the money lasts.
Advisor: Absolutely. And going back to what you just said—making it last—let’s start with your cash flow. Roughly, what’s your total gross income for the year?
Client: I think it’s around seventy-five thousand a year? Somewhere in there.

Advisor: Okay, about $735 a month. And the other pension—weekly?
Client: Yes, weekly. That one is about thirteen-fifty a week.
Advisor: Just to make sure I’m hearing you right—$1,350 each week from the weekly pension?
Client: Yes, that’s right.
Advisor: Perfect. Now expenses—what do you spend in a typical month, all-in? Mortgage or rent, utilities, food, insurance, the whole thing.

Advisor: Okay. Quick admin thing—Jennifer, I’m going to confirm how I’m labeling you in my notes: you’re Client 1, correct?
Client: Yes, I’m Client 1.
Advisor: Great. Now, I want to make sure I’m not using jargon—when I say “market,” I mean the channel you’re working with, like a U.S. registered investment advisor setup versus, say, a bank branch or insurance-only model.
Client: Oh—okay. I thought you meant like the stock market.
Advisor: Totally fair—most people do. In your case, we’re operating in the US RIA world—does that sound right to you?

Advisor: Okay—when you say 22%, are you thinking of what gets withheld, or your actual bracket when you file?
Client: Withheld, probably. I’m sorry, I mix that up.
Advisor: No, totally normal. Withholding is what comes out of the checks; bracket is the marginal rate on the last dollars. Do you recall seeing 32% anywhere on your return or tax software?
Client: Oh—yes. That sounds familiar. I think it is 32%, not 22.
Advisor: Okay, thank you—so we’ll use 32% as your bracket band.

Advisor: Walk me through them—one at a time. What’s the first one like, monthly or weekly?
Client: One is monthly. It’s about seven hundred and thirty-something a month.
Advisor: Okay, about $735 a month. And the other pension—weekly?
Client: Yes, weekly. That one is about thirteen-fifty a week.
Advisor: Just to make sure I’m hearing you right—$1,350 each week from the weekly pension?

Advisor: Okay. Let’s shift to what you’ve saved. Roughly, what do you have in investable accounts—so not counting the house—across bank, brokerage, retirement accounts?
Client: Um… maybe like five hundred thousand? A little over?
Advisor: A little over five hundred—okay. If you had to put a number on it, is it closer to $520,000 or closer to $500,000?
Client: Closer to five-seventeen, I think. About five hundred seventeen thousand, five hundred.

Client: July 5th… 1953.
Advisor: July 5, 1953—thank you. I’m going to repeat it back because I’ve fat-fingered dates before: 7/5/1953.
Client: Yes, that’s right.
Advisor: Great. And earlier you mentioned your sister with the storm—sorry, quick aside—are you the one doing all the finances now since your husband passed?

Client: Oh, I’m okay. A little tired, honestly, but I’m glad to get this organized.
Advisor: I hear you. We’ll keep it simple and step-by-step. Tell me a bit about you and what you want help with.

Client: I think it’s around seventy-five thousand a year? Somewhere in there.

Advisor: Around $78,150 in private markets, held through a bank channel—thank you. We can talk through what could go wrong there and how liquid it is.
Client: Please, yes.
Advisor: And then your home—just so we have the full picture—what’s the approximate value of your primary residence?
Client: About three twenty-five, maybe three twenty-six. Around three hundred twenty-five thousand.

Client: Uh… I think it’s like nine thousand a year? Something like that.
Advisor: Okay—around $9,000 a year. Hang on—do you remember if it’s closer to $8,000 or closer to $10,000?
Client: Closer to nine. I want to say $8,850 for the year, if I’m remembering right.
Advisor: Perfect, $8,850 a year for that monthly pension. Thank you.
Client: Yeah.

Advisor: Got it, about $79,000 gross annually. And that’s coming from pensions, right?
Client: Yes, two pensions.

Advisor: Perfect, $8,850 a year for that monthly pension. Thank you.
Client: Yeah.
Advisor: And just so I’m not using the wrong label—this is pension income, right? Not Social Security, not an annuity you bought—an actual pension.
Client: Right, it’s a pension.
Advisor: Got it. And the owner on that is you, Jennifer—paid to you personally.

Advisor: Yeah, that tracks with what you’ve been saying. When markets drop, you’d rather protect principal than chase returns, right?
Client: Exactly. I’d rather sleep at night.

Advisor: No, you’re consistent, which is helpful. So for our purposes we want the gross annualized number. Do you remember the gross annual amount for that weekly pension?
Client: I think it’s about seventy thousand a year.
Advisor: About seventy thousand—okay. I have a specific figure in mind from what you shared earlier, but I don’t want to put words in your mouth. Is it closer to $70,200 gross for the year?
Client: Yes—$70,200. That sounds right.
Advisor: Great, thank you. Weekly pension income, $70,200 gross annualized, owned by you.

Client: Yes, that’s fine. About three hundred twenty-five, eight-fifty—close enough.
Advisor: Okay. And just to double-check—no liens or anything that would mean money owed against the property?
Client: No. None.
Advisor: Alright. I might be mixing terms—when you said earlier “cards are paid off,” I took that as no debt outstanding. That’s right, yeah?

Advisor: About $280,150 in a taxable bank account—okay. Next, retirement: any IRA or old 401(k) held at a brokerage?
Client: Yes, there’s an IRA—well, I call it an IRA—at a brokerage. About one hundred three thousand.
Advisor: About $103,050 in the IRA/401k bucket at a brokerage. Then you mentioned something that’s more like an insurance company account—does that ring a bell?
Client: Yes, it’s through an insurance company. It’s sort of like a bank account—honestly I don’t totally get it.
Advisor: That’s okay. When you say “bank account,” do you mean it behaves like cash—stable value—or is it invested and can go up and down?

Advisor: Great—so monthly debt cost is $0. Any other loans at all? Personal loan, home equity line?
Client: No, none.
